question: What is .NET?
answerPoints: .NET is a free, open-source development platform made by Microsoft
It lets you build many types of applications — web, desktop, mobile, cloud, and more
It provides a runtime (CLR) that executes your code
It also provides a large library of pre-built functionality called the BCL (Base Class Library)
Languages like C#, VB.NET, and F# all run on .NET and can work together

question: What languages can you use with .NET?
answerPoints: C# is the most common and widely used .NET language
VB.NET (Visual Basic) is another option, historically popular in enterprise
F# is a functional-first language also supported on .NET
All .NET languages compile to the same intermediate language (IL), which is why they can interoperate

question: How can different .NET languages interoperate?
answerPoints: All .NET languages compile down to the same Intermediate Language (IL)
The CLR executes that IL regardless of which language wrote it
This means a C# class can inherit from a VB.NET class, for example
The Common Type System (CTS) ensures types are compatible across languages

question: What is a namespace?
answerPoints: A namespace is a way to organize and group related classes and types
It prevents naming conflicts between classes that share the same name in different libraries
Declared with the 'namespace' keyword
You access types using 'using' at the top of a file, or the fully qualified name (e.g. System.Collections.Generic.List)

question: Can you have multiple namespaces in one assembly?
answerPoints: Yes, a single assembly (.dll or .exe) can contain multiple namespaces
Namespaces are a logical grouping — assemblies are a physical deployment unit
They are independent concepts and don't have to match
Example: a single dll might contain MyApp.Data, MyApp.Services, and MyApp.Models

question: What is the difference between a value type and a reference type?
answerPoints: Value types store their data directly — examples: int, float, bool, struct
Reference types store a reference (pointer) to where the data lives in memory — examples: class, string, array
Value types typically live on the stack; reference types live on the heap
Assigning a value type copies the value; assigning a reference type copies the reference, not the data

question: Give examples of value types in C#
answerPoints: int, long, short, byte — integer types
float, double, decimal — floating point types
bool — true/false
char — a single character
struct — any custom struct you define
enum — enumerations are also value types

question: Give examples of reference types in C#
answerPoints: class — any class you define
string — even though it behaves like a value type due to immutability
array — even arrays of value types are reference types
interface, delegate — both are reference types
object — the base type of all types in C#

question: What is the difference between Parse and TryParse?
answerPoints: Parse converts a string to a type but throws an exception if the string is invalid
TryParse attempts the conversion and returns true/false instead of throwing
TryParse is safer when the input might not be valid
TryParse outputs the result via an out parameter: int.TryParse("5", out int result)

question: What is variable scope?
answerPoints: Scope refers to where in the code a variable can be accessed
A variable declared inside a method is only accessible within that method
A variable declared inside an if block or loop is only accessible inside that block
Class-level fields are accessible throughout the class
Accessing a variable outside its scope causes a compile error

question: What is the var keyword used for?
answerPoints: var lets the compiler infer the type of a variable from what's assigned to it
It is still strongly typed — the type is determined at compile time, not runtime
It can only be used for local variables, not class fields or method parameters
The variable must be initialized at declaration so the compiler can infer the type

question: What is the advantage of using the var keyword?
answerPoints: Reduces verbosity, especially with long generic types like Dictionary<string, List<int>>
Makes code easier to read when the type is obvious from context
Useful when the exact type is complex or anonymous (e.g. LINQ projections)
No runtime performance difference — it's purely a compile-time convenience

question: Explain the compilation cycle in .NET
answerPoints: You write C# source code (.cs files)
The C# compiler (Roslyn) compiles it into Intermediate Language (IL) packaged in an assembly (.dll or .exe)
When you run the app, the CLR's JIT compiler converts IL into native machine code
JIT compilation happens at runtime, allowing .NET to optimize for the specific machine
The native code is then executed by the CPU

question: What is an array?
answerPoints: An array is a fixed-size collection of elements of the same type
Elements are accessed by index (zero-based)
Once created, the size cannot change
Arrays are reference types even when they hold value types

question: What are the disadvantages of arrays?
answerPoints: Fixed size — you can't add or remove elements after creation
Inserting or deleting in the middle requires shifting all other elements manually
No built-in search, sort, or filter methods (compared to collections)
For dynamic sizing or richer functionality, List<T> is preferred

question: What are collection classes?
answerPoints: Collection classes are built-in classes for storing and managing groups of objects
Unlike arrays, most collections are dynamic — they resize automatically
They provide built-in methods for adding, removing, searching, and sorting
Found in System.Collections.Generic namespace

question: What are the advantages of collection classes over arrays?
answerPoints: Dynamic sizing — they grow and shrink automatically
Rich built-in methods: Add, Remove, Contains, Sort, etc.
Generic versions are type-safe and avoid boxing/unboxing
Specialized collections for different needs: Dictionary for lookups, Queue for FIFO, Stack for LIFO

question: Name common collection classes in C#
answerPoints: List<T> — dynamic array, most commonly used collection
Dictionary<TKey, TValue> — key/value pairs, fast lookups
HashSet<T> — unique values only, fast membership checks
Queue<T> — first in, first out (FIFO)
Stack<T> — last in, first out (LIFO)
LinkedList<T> — efficient insert/delete at any position

question: What is a runtime exception?
answerPoints: A runtime exception is an error that occurs while the program is running, not during compilation
The compiler cannot catch these — they only appear when the code actually executes
Common examples: NullReferenceException, IndexOutOfRangeException, DivideByZeroException
Runtime exceptions can be caught and handled using try/catch blocks

question: Give an example scenario that causes a NullReferenceException
answerPoints: Calling a method on an object that hasn't been initialized: Person p = null; p.GetName();
The compiler won't catch this — it only fails at runtime when the null is dereferenced
Fix: check for null before using the object, or ensure it's initialized
Null-conditional operator helps: p?.GetName() returns null instead of throwing

question: What is a finally block and why is it needed?
answerPoints: The finally block always runs after a try/catch, whether or not an exception occurred
Used to clean up resources that must be released regardless of success or failure
Common use: closing a file, database connection, or releasing a lock
Without finally, an exception thrown before cleanup code runs could leak resources
The 'using' statement is often a cleaner alternative for IDisposable resources

question: What is a struct?
answerPoints: A struct is a value type that can contain fields, properties, and methods
Unlike a class, it is copied when assigned — not shared by reference
Good for small, simple data containers like Point(x, y) or Color(r, g, b)
Structs cannot inherit from other structs or classes (but can implement interfaces)

question: What are the differences between a struct and a class?
answerPoints: Struct is a value type; class is a reference type
Structs are copied on assignment; classes share a reference
Structs typically live on the stack; classes live on the heap
Classes support inheritance; structs do not
Use structs for small, immutable data; use classes for complex objects with behavior

question: What access specifier should be used for fields in a class or struct?
answerPoints: Fields should generally be private — this is encapsulation
Expose data through public properties instead of public fields
Private fields are often prefixed with an underscore: private string _name;
This gives you control over how data is read and written

question: What are auto properties?
answerPoints: Auto properties let you define a property without writing a backing field manually
The compiler generates the backing field automatically
Basic syntax: public string Name { get; set; }
Read-only: public string Name { get; }
Init-only (C# 9+): public string Name { get; init; } — settable only during object construction

question: What are the four object-oriented principles?
answerPoints: Encapsulation: hiding internal data and exposing only what's needed via public methods/properties
Inheritance: a class can derive from another class and reuse/extend its behavior
Polymorphism: the same method call can behave differently depending on the object type
Abstraction: exposing only relevant details and hiding complexity behind a simple interface

question: What is encapsulation?
answerPoints: Encapsulation means hiding the internal state of an object and only exposing what's necessary
Fields are kept private; access is provided through public properties or methods
Protects data from being changed in unexpected ways
Makes code easier to maintain — internals can change without affecting outside code

question: What is inheritance?
answerPoints: Inheritance allows a class to derive from another class and reuse its behavior
The derived class gets all public and protected members of the base class
The derived class can override base class methods to change behavior
C# only supports single inheritance for classes — a class can only have one base class
Use the : symbol: public class Dog : Animal { }

question: What is polymorphism?
answerPoints: Polymorphism means the same method call can behave differently depending on the actual object type
A base class reference can point to a derived class object
When you call a virtual method, the derived class version runs — not the base class version
Example: Animal a = new Dog(); a.Speak(); — calls Dog's Speak(), not Animal's
Enables writing code that works with many types through a common interface

question: What is abstraction?
answerPoints: Abstraction means hiding complexity and exposing only what's relevant to the caller
You interact with an object through a simple interface without knowing how it works internally
Example: you call car.Start() without knowing about fuel injection, ignition timing, etc.
Achieved in C# through abstract classes and interfaces

question: What keyword is used to call base class functionality from a derived class?
answerPoints: The 'base' keyword is used to access the base class
Call a base class method: base.MethodName()
Call a base class constructor: public Dog(string name) : base(name) { }
Useful when overriding a method but still wanting the base behavior to run

question: What is an interface?
answerPoints: An interface defines a contract — a set of methods and properties a class must implement
Interfaces contain no implementation, only signatures (pre C# 8)
A class can implement multiple interfaces, unlike class inheritance which is single
Interfaces enable loose coupling — code depends on the interface, not the concrete class
This is the foundation of dependency injection and testability

question: What are the differences between an interface and an abstract class?
answerPoints: An abstract class can have implemented methods; an interface traditionally cannot (pre C# 8)
A class can implement multiple interfaces but can only inherit one abstract class
Abstract classes can have constructors, fields, and state; interfaces cannot
Use an abstract class when classes share common implementation
Use an interface to define a contract for loose coupling and dependency injection

question: What is the sealed keyword used for?
answerPoints: sealed on a class prevents it from being inherited
sealed on a method override prevents further overriding in derived classes
Use it when you want to lock down behavior and prevent unintended extension
string is an example of a sealed class in .NET

question: What is function overloading?
answerPoints: Overloading means defining multiple methods with the same name but different parameters
The compiler picks the right version based on the arguments passed
Parameters must differ in type, number, or order — return type alone is not enough
Example: Console.WriteLine() has many overloads accepting string, int, object, etc.

question: What is operator overloading?
answerPoints: Operator overloading lets you define custom behavior for operators like +, -, ==, > on your own types
Defined using the 'operator' keyword in a class or struct
Example: a Vector class could overload + to add two vectors component by component
Not all operators can be overloaded — =, &&, ||, ?? cannot
Use sparingly — non-obvious operator overloads make code harder to read

question: What are delegates?
answerPoints: A delegate is a type that holds a reference to a method with a specific signature
Think of it as a type-safe function pointer
Delegates allow methods to be passed as parameters or stored as variables
They are the foundation for events and LINQ in C#
Common built-in delegates: Action (no return), Func (has return), Predicate (returns bool)

question: What are events in C#?
answerPoints: An event is a way for a class to notify other classes when something happens
Events are based on delegates — the event defines the delegate signature subscribers must match
A class subscribes using += and unsubscribes with -=
To raise an event: MyEvent?.Invoke(this, args)
Common pattern: UI button clicks, data change notifications

question: How do generics improve performance?
answerPoints: Before generics, collections stored objects as 'object' type, requiring boxing/unboxing for value types
Boxing wraps a value type in an object on the heap — slow and creates GC pressure
Generics avoid boxing by generating type-specific code at compile/JIT time
Example: List<int> is much more efficient than ArrayList storing ints as objects
Generics also provide compile-time type safety — mistakes are caught before runtime

question: What is the difference between a .dll and an .exe?
answerPoints: An .exe is an executable — it has an entry point (Main method) and can be run directly
A .dll is a library — it contains reusable code but cannot run on its own
An .exe typically references one or more .dlls
In .NET, both are assemblies containing IL code — the difference is the entry point and how they're used

question: What is multitasking in .NET?
answerPoints: Multitasking is the ability to run multiple operations concurrently
In .NET, the Task class (System.Threading.Tasks) represents an asynchronous operation
The ThreadPool manages the threads that execute tasks behind the scenes
Tasks can be awaited using the await keyword, which pauses the method without blocking the thread

question: What is the purpose of async and await?
answerPoints: async marks a method as asynchronous — it can contain await expressions
await pauses the method until the awaited task completes, without blocking the thread
This frees the thread to do other work while waiting (e.g. for a database call or HTTP request)
The method resumes after the awaited task finishes
async methods typically return Task or Task<T> instead of void or a value type

question: What is Reflection in .NET?
answerPoints: Reflection allows you to inspect and interact with type metadata at runtime
You can discover the methods, properties, fields, and attributes of any type
You can also create instances and invoke methods dynamically
Useful for serialization, dependency injection frameworks, and plugin architectures
Key classes: Type, MethodInfo, PropertyInfo, Assembly (all in System.Reflection)
Downside: slower than direct code and loses compile-time type safety

question: What is deferred query execution in LINQ?
answerPoints: Deferred execution means a LINQ query is not run when it's defined — it runs when you iterate over it
The query is just a description of what to do, not the result
This allows queries to be composed and modified before execution
Execution is triggered by iterating (foreach) or calling ToList(), First(), Count(), etc.

question: What is forced query execution in LINQ and how do you achieve it?
answerPoints: Forced execution means running the query immediately and storing the result
Achieved by calling ToList(), ToArray(), ToDictionary(), First(), Count(), etc.
The result is a snapshot — changes to the source after this point are not reflected
Use forced execution when you need to iterate multiple times or pass a concrete list around

question: What are extension methods?
answerPoints: Extension methods let you add new methods to an existing type without modifying or inheriting from it
Must be in a static class and must be static methods
The first parameter uses the 'this' keyword followed by the type being extended
They appear as instance methods on the type when the namespace is imported
LINQ is entirely built on extension methods on IEnumerable<T>

question: What is Entity Framework?
answerPoints: Entity Framework (EF) is an ORM — Object Relational Mapper — for .NET
It lets you work with a database using C# objects instead of writing raw SQL
Supports LINQ queries that get translated to SQL automatically
Code-first approach: database schema is generated from your C# classes
EF Core is the modern cross-platform version

question: What are the advantages of Entity Framework?
answerPoints: Reduces boilerplate data access code — no manual SQL for basic CRUD
Type-safe queries with LINQ instead of raw SQL strings
Database schema can be generated and migrated from code
Supports multiple databases with minimal code changes
Change tracking automatically detects which objects need to be saved

question: What is implicit type conversion?
answerPoints: Implicit conversion happens automatically when there is no risk of data loss
Example: int to long — a long can always hold an int value
The compiler handles it without any extra syntax
Other examples: float to double, int to float

question: What is explicit type conversion?
answerPoints: Explicit conversion is required when data loss is possible
You must write a cast to tell the compiler you accept the risk
Syntax: int x = (int)myDouble; — decimal portion is truncated
If a cast fails at runtime it throws an InvalidCastException
Use 'as' for safe reference type casting — returns null instead of throwing

question: What is the advantage of StringBuilder over string concatenation?
answerPoints: Strings in C# are immutable — every + concatenation creates a new string object
In a loop, this creates many short-lived objects and GC pressure
StringBuilder maintains a mutable buffer and only allocates when needed
For building strings in loops or with many appends, StringBuilder is significantly faster
For simple one-or-two concatenations, + or string interpolation is fine

question: What is the lock keyword used for?
answerPoints: lock ensures only one thread can execute a block of code at a time
Prevents race conditions when multiple threads access shared data
The lock object should be private and dedicated — typically a readonly object field
Overuse of lock can cause deadlocks or reduce performance

question: What are ref parameters?
answerPoints: ref allows a method to modify the caller's variable directly
The variable must be initialized before being passed in
Both the caller and the method must use the ref keyword
Less common in modern C# where return values and tuples are preferred

question: What are out parameters?
answerPoints: out allows a method to return additional values through parameters
The variable does not need to be initialized before being passed in
The method must assign the out parameter before returning
Most common use: TryParse pattern — bool success = int.TryParse(s, out int result)

question: What are optional parameters?
answerPoints: Optional parameters have a default value and don't need to be passed by the caller
Declared by assigning a default in the method signature: void Greet(string name = "World")
Optional parameters must come after required parameters
Callers can omit them or pass a value to override the default

question: What are named parameters?
answerPoints: Named parameters let you specify arguments by parameter name instead of position
Syntax: Greet(name: "Alice", greeting: "Hello")
Improves readability when a method has many parameters
Allows you to pass optional parameters out of order

question: What is a data structure?
answerPoints: A data structure is a way of organizing and storing data so it can be accessed and modified efficiently
Different structures are optimized for different operations — searching, inserting, sorting, etc.
Choosing the right data structure is a key part of writing efficient code
Examples: array, list, stack, queue, dictionary, tree, graph

question: What is a stack data structure?
answerPoints: A stack is a Last In, First Out (LIFO) structure
The last item added is the first one removed
Main operations: Push (add to top), Pop (remove from top), Peek (view top without removing)
Real-world analogy: a stack of plates — you add and remove from the top
Common uses: undo history, call stack, expression parsing

question: What is a queue data structure?
answerPoints: A queue is a First In, First Out (FIFO) structure
The first item added is the first one removed
Main operations: Enqueue (add to back), Dequeue (remove from front), Peek (view front)
Real-world analogy: a line at a store — first person in line is first served
Common uses: task scheduling, message queues, breadth-first search

question: What is a dictionary data structure?
answerPoints: A dictionary stores data as key/value pairs
Each key must be unique; values can be duplicated
Lookup by key is very fast — O(1) average time
In C#: Dictionary<TKey, TValue>
Common uses: caching, lookups by ID, counting occurrences

question: What is a linked list?
answerPoints: A linked list is a collection where each element (node) holds data and a reference to the next node
Unlike arrays, elements are not stored in contiguous memory
Inserting or deleting at any position is efficient — just update the references
Random access is slow — you must traverse from the start to reach a specific index
In C#: LinkedList<T>

question: What is a hash set?
answerPoints: A hash set stores unique values only — duplicates are automatically ignored
Checking whether a value exists is very fast — O(1) average
No guaranteed order of elements
In C#: HashSet<T>
Common uses: deduplication, fast membership checks

question: What is a binary tree?
answerPoints: A binary tree is a structure where each node has at most two children: left and right
The topmost node is called the root
Leaf nodes have no children
Binary Search Trees (BST) keep left children smaller and right children larger than the parent
BST lookup, insert, and delete are O(log n) on average for balanced trees

question: What is Big O notation?
answerPoints: Big O notation describes how the performance of an algorithm scales with input size
It describes the worst-case scenario
O(1) — constant time: performance doesn't change regardless of input size
O(n) — linear time: performance grows proportionally with input size
O(n²) — quadratic: performance grows with the square of input size (e.g. nested loops)
O(log n) — logarithmic: performance grows slowly, common in search trees and binary search

question: What is the time complexity of common List<T> operations?
answerPoints: Add to end: O(1) amortized — occasionally resizes the internal array
Insert at index: O(n) — must shift all elements after the insertion point
Remove at index: O(n) — must shift elements to fill the gap
Access by index: O(1) — direct memory access
Search (Contains): O(n) — must scan the whole list in the worst case

question: What is the time complexity of Dictionary<TKey,TValue> operations?
answerPoints: Lookup by key: O(1) average — uses a hash function to find the bucket
Insert: O(1) average
Delete: O(1) average
Worst case is O(n) if many keys hash to the same bucket (hash collision)
In practice, .NET's Dictionary implementation handles collisions well

question: What is the difference between a shallow copy and a deep copy?
answerPoints: A shallow copy copies the object but not the objects it references — nested objects are shared
A deep copy copies the object and all objects it references — fully independent
With a shallow copy, modifying a nested object in the copy affects the original too
Deep copies are more expensive but safer when you need true independence
In C#, MemberwiseClone() produces a shallow copy

question: What is boxing and unboxing?
answerPoints: Boxing is wrapping a value type in an object reference so it can be stored on the heap
Example: object o = 42; — the int 42 is boxed into an object
Unboxing is extracting the value type back out: int x = (int)o;
Boxing/unboxing has a performance cost — heap allocation and casting
Generics were introduced largely to avoid boxing in collections
